elements.sent-2411-001: Uranium mononitride (UN) is a promising accident tolerant fuel due to its high fissile density and high thermal conductivity.
elements.sent-2411-002: In this study, we developed the first machine learning interatomic potentials for reliable atomic-scale modeling of UN at finite temperatures.
elements.sent-2411-003: We constructed a training set using density functional theory (DFT) calculations that was enriched through an active learning procedure and two neural network potentials were generated.
elements.sent-2411-004: We found that both potentials can reproduce some thermophysical properties of interest such as temperature dependent heat capacity.
elements.sent-2411-005: We also evaluated the energy of stoichiometric defect reactions and defect migration barriers and found close agreement with DFT values demonstrating that our potentials can be used for a description of defects in UN.
elements.sent-2411-006: The increasing demand for nuclear energy that meets the safety requirements for operation has brought attention to uranium mononitride (UN) since its identification as a potential accident tolerant fuel.
elements.sent-2411-007: An accident tolerant fuel is characterized by its ability to tolerate accidents involving a loss of coolant for a period longer than the benchmark standard UO$_2$.
elements.sent-2411-008: This is made possible by achieving improved fuel properties such as high thermal conductivity, which lowers fuel center-line temperatures, and increased melt temperatures \cite{nea2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-009: It has been reported that UN has a higher thermal conductivity and higher melting temperature compared to UO$_2$, and it is compatible with cladding alloys, as well as generally low rates of fission gas release \cite{Matthews1993, Watkins2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: Despite these promising qualities, licensing a new accident tolerant fuel requires a comprehensive understanding of the fuel, especially under realistic operating conditions to address the full range of material responses, making characterizations and experimental measurements crucial.
elements.sent-2411-011: For this purpose, multiscale multiphysics fuel performance models have become more popular as they can provide insights on the evolution of nuclear fuels in a reactor, drastically reducing the experimental measurements needed --- a valuable point when there is already limited experimental data like in the UN case.
elements.sent-2411-012: These models, however, require input parameters from lower-length scale simulation methods like density functional theory (DFT) and molecular dynamics (MD) that can provide an accurate description of the material at the atomistic level.
elements.sent-2411-013: Many DFT studies have been used to predict the electronic structure and magnetic ordering of UN \cite{Gryaznov2012, Mei2013, Kocevski2022a , and used to understand point defects and incorporation energies of fission products \cite{Kotomin2009, Ducher2011, Claisse2016, Szpunar2020, Yang2021, Zhao2023, Kuganathan2023 , as they can accommodate changes in stoichiometry facilitating fission product migration through the fuel, one of the main degradation mechanisms \cite{Tonks2018 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: While these calculations were able to identify relevant UN behavior, DFT is computationally demanding, making classical MD simulations preferable in most cases, as it can offer more flexibility in predicting fuel behavior at finite temperatures and drastically longer time scales.
elements.sent-2411-015: However, this comes with a caveat, the accuracy of classical MD predictions is highly dependent on the interatomic potentials used during the simulation.
elements.sent-2411-016: For UN, several classical interatomic potentials have been developed \cite{AbdulHameed2024 with moderate accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Kuksin et al. \cite{Kuksin2016 developed an angular-dependent potential fitted to DFT calculations.}
elements.sent-2411-018: This potential has been subsequently modified by Tseplyaev and Starikov to calculate point defect formation and migration energies \cite{Tseplyaev2016 and diffusion in hypo-stoichiometric UN \cite{Li2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-019: A modified version of this potential was later used to simulate the growth of Xenon bubbles in UN \cite{Li2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Kocevski et al. \cite{Kocevski2022 developed an embedded-atom method potential that can reproduce various thermal dependent properties.}
elements.sent-2411-021: This model was used to investigate Xenon diffusion in UN, although it underestimates heat capacity at high temperatures.
elements.sent-2411-022: As several differences arise between the available interatomic potentials, a reliable UN potential is a necessity.
elements.sent-2411-023: Recently, machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) have been successfully employed to predict properties of nuclear fuels.
elements.sent-2411-024: Chen et al. applied a moment tensor potential to train a uranium MLIP, capable of predicting multiple uranium crystal phases \cite{Chen2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-025: Stippell et al. trained an MLIP for UO$_2$, showing the additional complexity introduced by DFT+U calculations \cite{Stippell2024 and moderately reproducing several temperature dependent properties and reporting stoichiometric point defects.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Similarly, Dubois et al. presented a MLIP to improve the description of the elastic and plastic behavior of UO$_2$ \cite{Dubois2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: While promising, these studies highlight the challenges present when constructing MLIPs to model actinide systems.
elements.sent-2411-028: In this study, we have developed two MLIPs for UN, leveraging the accurate neural network engine for molecular energies (ANI) \cite{Smith2017 and the hierarchically interacting particle neural network (HIP-NN) \cite{Lubbers2018, Chigaev2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-029: We employ state-of-the-art DFT calculations on actinide nitrides with an active-learning methodology to ensure an efficient sampling by enabling the generation of a diverse dataset for training that contains defect structures.
elements.sent-2411-030: First, in Section II, we provide a brief description of the computational methods used to develop the MLIPs.
elements.sent-2411-031: Then in Section III, we assess the performance of the trained potentials by evaluating thermomechanical properties (including their temperature dependence), defect formation energies, phonon modes, transport properties and migration barriers.
elements.sent-2411-032: We present two MLIPs for UN, an ANI-based \cite{Smith2017 potential and a a HIP-NN-based \cite{Lubbers2018, Chigaev2023 potential.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Recent studies have shown that HIP-NN can outperform ANI \cite{Chigaev2023, Fedik2024 , which provides a solid baseline \cite{Smith2020, Smith2021, Stippell2024 , for a modern machine learned potential.}}
elements.sent-2411-034: We examine both models in search of the potential with best performance across a wide range of materials properties.
elements.sent-2411-035: Both MLIPs predict atomic energies, where the total system energy is the sum of all atomic energies; this approximation is common although not entirely physical.
elements.sent-2411-036: The forces are computed as the gradient of the energy with respect to positions by automatic differentiation, thus guaranteeing that the potential is energy-conserving.
elements.sent-2411-037: ANI and HIP-NN are local, meaning they only include interactions within a specified cutoff radius.
elements.sent-2411-038: Both model architectures are constructed to ensure that the total energy and atomic forces are invariant to translations, rotations, and permutations.. ANI potential.
elements.sent-2411-039: ANI is a prototype of a Behler-Parinello type \cite{Behler2007 neural-network potential.}
elements.sent-2411-040: These types of potentials use radial and angular symmetry functions which probe the atomic environment of each atom, providing descriptors, or features, for each atom that measure statistical properties of its environment in a way that is invariant to the fundamental symmetries (rotation, translation, and atomic permutation) of atomistic systems.
elements.sent-2411-041: These descriptors are then fed into feed forward neural networks to predict atomic energies and forces based on radial and angular atomic descriptors.
elements.sent-2411-042: While the radial atomic descriptors are identical to those of Behler and Parrinello, ANI utilizes modified angular symmetry functions designed to improve angular information gathering.
elements.sent-2411-043: Furthermore, ANI uses a unique set of learned parameters for each type of element in the system; there is one neural net model for each type of atom in the potential.
elements.sent-2411-044: Thus, in this work, two separate neural networks are trained, one for uranium atoms, and one for nitrogen atoms.
elements.sent-2411-045: A recently published work demonstrated the success of modeling UO$_2$ with an ANI potential \cite{Stippell2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-046: In the present work, we follow the same hyperparameters (principally, the network size parameters) used in the UO$_2$ potential, where the oxygen hyperparameters were assigned to the nitrogen neural network.. HIP-NN potential.
elements.sent-2411-047: HIP-NN, in contrast to ANI, is a prototypical example of an end-to-end message-passing neural network potential \cite{Kocer2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-048: Instead of using pre-defined symmetry functions to describe the atomic environment, the characterization of the environment takes place through learned functions.
elements.sent-2411-049: In HIP-NN, this learning uses sensitivity functions ($s$) which characterize distances between atoms, and interaction weights ($V$) which combine the distances and weights of neighboring atoms to form a message.
elements.sent-2411-050: Sensitivity functions are indexed by $\nu$ which take pairwise distance as input, features $Z$ indexed by $a$ (output) and $b$ (input), and neighbors of atom $i$ indexed by $j$.
elements.sent-2411-051:
The crucial equation relating input features to the pre-activation of neurons, $A$, is  egin{equation}\label{e1} A_{i,b} = \sum_{
u,j,b} V^
u_{ab} s^
u(r_{ij}) Z_{j,b}\;.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-052: The relation in Eq.~(\ref{e1 ) demonstrates that the message received is a learned product reduction between the sensitivity functions and the features of neighbors, summed over all neighbors in the environment of $i$.}
elements.sent-2411-053: In contrast to the species-based symmetry functions of Behler-Parinello networks, this equation can be applied recursively using multiple interaction layers to generate deeper and longer-ranged interactions.
elements.sent-2411-054: However, in this work we found that one interaction layer was sufficient ($n_interaction=1$).
elements.sent-2411-055: We used $n_feature=60$ neurons per layer and $n_\nu=20$ sensitivity functions.
elements.sent-2411-056: We used the improved interaction form, HIP-NN with tensor sensitivities \cite{Chigaev2023 with tensor order ${\ell =1$, that is, vector-based message passing, which improves over the original HIP-NN by summing messages generated from neighbors not only as scalars, but also as vectors.}}
elements.sent-2411-057: This allows individual neurons to be sensitive to angles between neighbors.
elements.sent-2411-058: We used the open-source hippynn \cite{hippynn implementation of HIP-NN for this work, which provides not only functions for learning, but also an interface to molecular dynamics simulations with LAMMPS \cite{Thompson2022 .}}
elements.subsection-2411-002: Active learning procedure for dataset generation
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{atomicstructs.pdf} \caption{\label{fig:snaps} Snapshots of UN atomic configurations found in the training dataset consisting of (a) stable crystalline rocksalt structure found bootstrap dataset, (b) highly disordered structure from the last active learning iteration dataset, and (c) structure containing an uranium interstitial that is part of the last active learning iteration dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-059: Machine learning interatomic potentials rely on the quality and diversity of the training dataset.
elements.sent-2411-060: For this purpose, active learning strategies have been developed for efficiently building models and training datasets that can contain a wide range of configurations in a fully automated fashion.
elements.sent-2411-061: The objective of active learning is to find new configurations where the current model is deficient.
elements.sent-2411-062: These new atomic configurations are generated by using low cost sampling methods, usually by means of MD simulations.
elements.sent-2411-063: Subsequently, a subset of these configurations is selected for labeling, i.e. assigned DFT energies and forces, and added to the ever-growing dataset.
elements.sent-2411-064: A common strategy for selecting structures is called Query by committee, where an ensemble (or committee) of MLIP models are independently trained and the uncertainty of a prediction is quantified by the variance among the predictions on both energy and forces made by the ensemble of MLIPs.
elements.sent-2411-065: An initial database is constructed containing configurations of 64-atom supercells, including crystalline rocksalt (\hmn{fm-3m ) structures and small deformations around the stable structure \hmn{fm-3m .}}
elements.sent-2411-066: The active learning procedure is employed by performing single-point DFT calculations on randomly selected configurations included in the initial database.
elements.sent-2411-067: We then train an ensemble of eight MLIPs using the neural network ANI to carry out the Query by committee, where each ensemble member uses different random splits of training, validation and tests datasets.
elements.sent-2411-068: More details can be found in Ref. \cite{Smith2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-069: Sampling is achieved by running MD simulations using the newly-trained potentials at each iteration, where the temperature and box size is dynamically varied to diversify the sampled configurations.
elements.sent-2411-070: The active learning dataset is expanded on early iterations with configurations targeting specific defects, neutral Schottky defects and uranium and nitrogen Frenkel pairs.
elements.sent-2411-071: The final dataset contains 12336 atomic configurations and was used to train the second MLIP using the HIP-NN potential.
elements.sent-2411-072: All DFT calculations were performed using plane-wave-based Vienna ab initio simulation package (VASP 6.3.2).
elements.sent-2411-073: The generalized-gradient approximation (GGA) \cite{Perdew1996 with projected-augmented wave (PAW) \cite{Kresse1999 potentials was used to describe the exchange-correlation functional.}}
elements.sent-2411-074: Spin-polarized calculations with ferromagnetic (FM) ordering on uranium atoms was considered to model UN, as it is predicted to be the ground state by the PBE functional.
elements.sent-2411-075: Since the interatomic potentials are trained to forces, phonon dispersions were calculated to verify that the PBE remains the most suitable choice for labeling our UN training dataset, compared to DFT+$U$ \cite{Dudarev1998 and SCAN \cite{Furness2020 functionals (see Appendix \ref{app:dft ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-076: Labeling calculations were carried out on a $2 \times 2 \times 2$ supercell of the rocksalt unit cell with periodic boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-077: We set a cutoff energy of 520 eV to expand the Kohn-Sham orbitals into the plane-wave basis sets, and the Methfessel-Paxton smearing method with width 0.1 eV was used \cite{Methfessel1989 .}
elements.sent-2411-078: The energy and the force convergence criteria were set as $10^{-8}$ eV and $10^{-4}$ eV/\AA, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-079: A $3 \times 3 \times 3$ k-point mesh was used to sample the Brillouin zone.
elements.sent-2411-080: All MD calculations were performed using the Large-scale Atomic/Molecular Massively Parallel Simulator (LAMMPS) software package \cite{Thompson2022 using periodic boundary conditions in all directions.}
elements.sent-2411-081: Elastic tensor at 0 K were calculated on a $5 \times 5 \times 5$ supercell using the deformation method.
elements.sent-2411-082: This size was also chosen to calculate point defect energies and migration barriers, which were obtained using the nudge elastic band method as implemented in LAMMPS.
elements.sent-2411-083: For temperature-dependent calculations we used a 15 $\times$ 15 $\times$ 15 supercell under the isobaric-isothermal ensemble NPT, and damping parameters of 0.1 ps and 0.5 ps using the Nos\'e–Hoover thermostat and barostat respectively.
elements.sent-2411-084: Time step was set to 1 fs.
elements.sent-2411-085: A series of simulations were performed for temperatures ranging from 300 K to 2700 K with a step of 25 K, at zero pressure for a duration of 30 ps.
elements.sent-2411-086: The lattice parameter was obtained directly from the simulation, averaging the last 10 ps, at the target temperature.
elements.sent-2411-087: The specific heat capacity was computed as the partial derivative of enthalpy, extracted from the last 10 ps of each run, with respect to temperature.
elements.sent-2411-088: The temperature dependent bulk modulus was also calculated as the derivative of pressure with respect to volume, all taken from the last 10 ps of a series of compressive and tensile strain simulations.
elements.sent-2411-089: The self-diffusion of nitrogen was obtained by extracting the mean square displacements from a 1 ns run under the microcanonical ensemble in a temperature range of 1800 K to 2700 K in 15 $\times$ 15 $\times$ 15 UN supercell.
elements.sent-2411-090: We performed the simulations of stoichometric and hypo-stoichometric (containing nitrogen vacancies and interstitals) systems after equilibrating for 20 ps using an NPT ensemble.
elements.sent-2411-091: We performed MD simulations of displacement cascades on the HIP-NN model.
elements.sent-2411-092: Collision cascade is triggered by assigning to a uranium atom as a primary knock-on atom (PKA), with a random direction and a kinetic energy of 1 keV.
elements.sent-2411-093: Initially the system was equilibrated with the NPT ensemble for 20 ps at zero pressure and 600 K.
elements.sent-2411-094: During collision, the simulation is performed in the NVE ensemble.
elements.sent-2411-095: Periodic boundary conditions were used for a box containing 80 $\times$ 80 $\times$ 80 conventional unit cells ($\sim$4 million atoms).
elements.sent-2411-096: The time step of this simulation was set to be dynamic, so no atom can move more than 0.05\AA, with a maximum of 1 fs.
elements.sent-2411-097: Visual post-processing was performed by Open Visualization Tool (OVITO) software \cite{ovito using the Wigner-Seitz cell method to identify point defects and non-defective lattice sites.}
elements.sent-2411-098: In order to evaluate the performance of the two potentials, we first looked at the energy and force RMSE of the ANI and HIP-NN potentials on the whole dataset as shown in Table \ref{tab:rmse .}
elements.sent-2411-099: These results are satisfactory, especially considering the dataset contains a diverse set of configurations sampled over a wide range of temperatures and densities.

elements.subsection-2411-006: Diffusion, migration barriers, and Xe incorporation energies
elements.sent-2411-122: To further understand irradiation behavior of a nuclear fuel, it is important to have accurate predictions of the self-diffusion and the incorporation energies of impurities such as xenon.
elements.sent-2411-123: For simplicity, only HIP-NN is employed to evaluate diffusion, migration barriers, and Xe incorporation energies.
elements.sent-2411-124: The self-diffusion of nitrogen in stoichiometric and hypo-stoichiometric systems containing nitrogen vacancies and interstitials was calculated using HIP-NN over a range of temperatures by monitoring the mean-square displacement and it is shown in Fig. \ref{fig:dif .}
elements.sent-2411-125: For a perfect UN, the diffusion can only be reported in a higher temperature regime, as diffusion timescales cannot be reached in our simulations.
elements.sent-2411-126: To study the effect of nitrogen vacancies, we simulated two hypo-stoichiometric structures, one with 5\% N vacancies and one with 1\% N vacancies and we found that as we add vacancies to UN, there is an increase diffusivity and decrease the diffusion activation energy, since migration of nitrogen atoms is assisted by the empty lattice sites.
elements.sent-2411-127: When calculating the diffusion on nitrogen interstitials (0.5\% N added), the diffusion values at the studied temperature range are further enhanced and the activation energy is significantly lower than the stoichiometric sample, showing that anion diffusion in UN is mainly interstitial as pointed out in experimental observations \cite{Holt1969, Matzke1990 .}

Error bars are standard deviation from the different ensembles.}
elements.sent-2411-128: The calculated migration barriers for U and N vacancy (vac) and interstitial (int) obtained from NEB are presented in Table \ref{tab:neb .}
elements.sent-2411-129: Due to the high temperature sampling in systems with defects, we expect to encounter some rare events, however we should point out that the training dataset does not contain structures from migration barrier calculations, although these could be included.
elements.sent-2411-130: Yet, HIP-NN is able to provide reliable predictions that are in moderate agreement with reported DFT calculations.

The HIP-NN xenon incorporation energies into an N vacancy (Xe$_\text{N}$) and a U vacancy (Xe$_\text{U}$) are in excellent agreement when compared to reference DFT data calculated using PBE \cite{Kocevski2022 .}

elements.sent-2411-137: Cascade modeling are of primary interest in the nuclear engineering community as they are major tools to analyze the behavior of materials submitted to internal or external irradiation.

elements.sent-2411-167: Uranium mononitride (UN) is a promising accident-tolerant fuel because of its high fissile density and high thermal conductivity.
elements.sent-2411-168: We constructed a training set using density functional theory (DFT) calculations that was enriched through an active learning procedure, and two neural network potentials were generated.
elements.sent-2411-169: Both potentials successfully reproduce key thermophysical properties of interest, such as temperature-dependent lattice parameter, specific heat capacity, and bulk modulus.
elements.sent-2411-170: We also evaluated the energy of stoichiometric defect reactions and defect migration barriers and found close agreement with DFT predictions, demonstrating that our potentials can be used for modeling defects in UN.
elements.sent-2411-171: Additional tests provide evidence that our potentials are reliable for simulating diffusion, noble gas impurities, and radiation damage.
elements.sent-2411-172: The growing demand for safer nuclear energy has intensified research efforts toward accident-tolerant fuels.
elements.sent-2411-173: Among these, uranium mononitride (UN) has emerged as a particularly promising candidate due to its superior properties, including higher fissile density, enhanced thermal conductivity, and higher melting temperature compared to conventional UO$_2$ fuels.
elements.sent-2411-174: These advantages directly translate into improved reactor safety, enabling fuels to better withstand accidents involving coolant loss by reducing fuel center-line temperatures and delaying fuel melting events~\cite{nea2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-175: Experimental evidence highlights several properties of UN, notably its compatibility with cladding materials and its relatively low rate of fission gas release compared to oxide fuels~\cite{Matthews1993, Watkins2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-176: However, the adoption and licensing of a new accident-tolerant fuel requires a thorough understanding of the fuel, especially under realistic operating conditions to address the full range of material responses, which often requires extensive characterizations and crucial experimental measurements.
elements.sent-2411-177: To address the challenge of limited experimental data, especially for novel fuels such as UN, multiscale multiphysics modeling frameworks have been increasingly utilized.
elements.sent-2411-178: These methods effectively reduce experimental workloads by leveraging computational tools to predict fuel performance over long operational durations.
elements.sent-2411-179: However, these models critically depend on accurate atomistic simulations, such as density functional theory (DFT) calculations and classical molecular dynamics (MD), to provide reliable estimates of material parameters.
elements.sent-2411-180: Numerous DFT studies have extensively investigated the electronic structure and magnetic properties of uranium mononitride (UN)~\cite{Gryaznov2012, Mei2013, Kocevski2022a .}
elements.sent-2411-181: In addition, substantial efforts have been made to understand point defect behavior and the incorporation of fission products into the UN\cite{Kotomin2009, Ducher2011, Claisse2016, Szpunar2020, Yang2021, Zhao2023, Kuganathan2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-182: These works have provided valuable insights into the mechanisms that govern fission gas migration and can further lead to fuel degradation.
elements.sent-2411-183: Yet, despite their accuracy, DFT calculations are computationally demanding and typically limited to short timescales and small system sizes.
elements.sent-2411-184: In contrast, classical MD simulations enable exploration of larger systems and longer simulation times, for better prediction of fuel behavior at finite temperatures, but their reliability heavily depends on the quality of the interatomic potentials employed.
elements.sent-2411-185: For UN, several classical interatomic potentials have been developed with moderate success~\cite{Kuksin2016, Tseplyaev2016, Kocevski2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-186: Kuksin et al.~\cite{Kuksin2016 developed an angular-dependent potential, later refined by Tseplyaev and Starikov~\cite{Tseplyaev2016 to study defects, migration energies, and pressure-induced \hmn{fm-3m $\rightarrow$ \hmn{R-3m phase transitions of UN.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-187: Li and Murphy~\cite{Li2021 applied this potential to study self-diffusion in hypo-stoichiometric conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-188: Another notable effort by Kocevski et al.~\cite{Kocevski2022 involved combining Buckingham-type interactions with the Finnis-Sinclair many-body embedded atom method potential, which can reproduce various thermal-dependent properties.}
elements.sent-2411-189: As several differences arise between the available interatomic potentials, a robust and reliable UN potential is a necessity.
elements.sent-2411-190: For instance, Chen et al. applied a moment tensor potential to train a uranium MLIP capable of capturing multiple crystal phases~\cite{Chen2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-191: Stippell et al. trained an MLIP for UO$_2$, showing the additional complexity introduced by DFT+U calculations~\cite{Stippell2024 and moderately reproducing several temperature-dependent properties and reporting stoichiometric point defects.}
elements.sent-2411-192: Dubois et al. demonstrated significant improvements in modeling the elastic and plastic behaviors of UO$_2$~\cite{Dubois2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-193: While these studies demonstrate great potential and outline novel strategies for generating training datasets and efficiently sampling defect configurations, they also underscore significant challenges associated with modeling actinide materials.
elements.sent-2411-194: These challenges include the high computational expense inherent in generating sufficiently large datasets and difficulties in achieving consistent convergence within first-principles calculations, particularly when dealing with strongly correlated electronic systems like those involving actinide elements.
elements.sent-2411-195: In this study, we have developed and validated two distinct MLIPs specifically tailored for UN: an accurate neural network engine for molecular energies (ANI) neural network potential~\cite{Smith2017 and a hierarchically interacting particle neural network (HIP-NN) potential~\cite{Lubbers2018, Chigaev2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-196: Using a state-of-the-art active learning approach, our methodology ensures comprehensive sampling of relevant atomic configurations, including point defects.
elements.sent-2411-197: The resulting potentials exhibit excellent agreement with the DFT calculations in several thermophysical and thermomechanical properties, such as temperature-dependent heat capacity, defect formation energies, and defect migration barriers.
elements.sent-2411-198: Our results establish a reliable foundation for further exploration of atomic-scale phenomena in UN that can improve nuclear fuel performance models.
elements.sent-2411-199: We developed two MLIPs for uranium mononitride: an ANI-based neural network potential~\cite{Smith2017 and a HIP-NN potential~\cite{Lubbers2018, Chigaev2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-200: In a previous study, the ANI potential was used to develop an MLIP for UO$_2$ and since it was linked to the active learning framework used in this work it was our first choice.
elements.sent-2411-201: However, recent studies have shown that HIP-NN can outperform ANI~\cite{Chigaev2023, Fedik2024 , therefore we decided to train a HIP-NN potential and examine the performance of both models across several material properties.}
elements.sent-2411-202: Similar to classical potentials, both MLIPs predict the total system energy as sums of atomic energies; atomic forces are obtained from gradients of the total energy with respect to atomic positions via automatic differentiation, ensuring energy conservation.
elements.sent-2411-203: Both ANI and HIP-NN potentials incorporate only local atomic interactions within specified cutoff radii and guarantee invariance to translations, rotations, and atomic permutations, enabling transferable and physically consistent modeling of atomic interactions.. ANI potential.
elements.sent-2411-204: ANI adopts the framework of Behler-Parinello neural network potentials~\cite{Behler2007 , utilizing radial and angular symmetry functions to characterize the local atomic environment of each atom.}
elements.sent-2411-205: Atomic environment vectors were constructed using both radial and angular symmetry functions.
elements.sent-2411-206: While the radial descriptors in ANI are consistent with the original Behler–Parrinello formulation, the angular symmetry functions are modified to improve the representation of local angular environments, thereby enhancing predictive accuracy for more complex atomic systems.
elements.sent-2411-207: In ANI, a separate neural network is trained for each atomic species, i.e., a separate neural network is trained for both uranium and nitrogen.
elements.sent-2411-208: The hyperparameters were adopted from prior optimization for UO$_2$~\cite{Stippell2024 , with the nitrogen-specific parameters adapted from those originally developed for oxygen.. HIP-NN potential.}
elements.sent-2411-209: HIP-NN utilizes an end-to-end, graph-based, message-passing neural network approach~\cite{Kocer2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-210: Instead of predefined symmetry functions to describe the atomic environment, the characterization of the environment takes place through learned functions.
elements.sent-2411-211: In HIP-NN, this learning uses sensitivity functions ($s$) which characterize distances between atoms, and interaction weights ($V$) combining the distances and weights of neighboring atoms to form a message.
elements.sent-2411-212: The sensitivity functions are indexed by $\nu$ and take pairwise distance as input, the feature vector $Z$ is indexed by $a$ (output) and $b$ (input), and the neighbors of atom $i$ indexed by $j$.
elements.sent-2411-213: The relation in Eq.~(\ref{e1 ) demonstrates that the message received is a learned-product reduction between the sensitivity functions and the features of neighbors, summed over all neighbors in the environment of $i$.}
elements.sent-2411-214: In contrast to the species-based symmetry functions of Behler-Parinello networks, this equation can be applied recursively using multiple interaction layers to generate deeper and longer-range interactions.
elements.sent-2411-215: We used the hippynn~\cite{hippynn open-source implementation of HIP-NN for this work, which provides not only functions for learning, but also an interface to molecular dynamics simulations with Large-scale Atomic/Molecular Massively Parallel Simulator (LAMMPS) package~\cite{Thompson2022 .}}

elements.sent-2411-217: The accuracy of MLIPs depends critically on the quality, diversity, and coverage of their training datasets.
elements.sent-2411-218: To construct such datasets efficiently, active learning strategies have been developed to autonomously explore chemical space and iteratively improve model performance.
elements.sent-2411-219: These approaches identify and incorporate atomic configurations where model predictions are uncertain or inaccurate, thus increasing the robustness of the model.
elements.sent-2411-220: New configurations are generated via low-cost sampling methods --typically MD simulations-- and a selected subset are then labeled using first-principles calculations to obtain reference energies and forces that will be added to the ever-growing dataset.
elements.sent-2411-221: A widely adopted selection strategy is query-by-committee (QBC), in which an ensemble of independently trained MLIP models is used to estimate uncertainty~\cite{Smith2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-222: For each candidate configuration, disagreement among the ensemble’s predictions is quantified on both the total energy and atomic forces.
elements.sent-2411-223: Initially, we created a bootstrap dataset containing configurations of 64-atom supercells, primarily in stable crystalline rock salt (\hmn{fm-3m ) structures as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:snaps (a), along with configurations involving small perturbations of the stable structure.}}
elements.sent-2411-224: Initial labeling was performed by selecting random configurations from this set and calculating their DFT energies and forces from static calculations.
elements.sent-2411-225: Additionally, to enhance diversity, configurations representing defect types--neutral Schottky defects, uranium and nitrogen Frenkel pairs--were manually added to the dataset in the early iterations of AL and subsequently used for sampling.
elements.sent-2411-226: Fig.~\ref{fig:snaps (b) shows an atomic snapshot of a structure with an uranium at an interstitial position, sampled in the last iteration of AL and included in the dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-227: Atomic configurations were sampled through MD simulations employing the MLIP ensemble for the current AL iteration, applying systematic temperature and density perturbations to explore a broad range of configurations, including non-equilibrium states relevant to high-temperature or high-pressure environments~\cite{Smith2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-228: Structures become increasingly disordered with AL iterations as can be seen from the snapshot in Fig.~\ref{fig:snaps (c) of a highly disordered configuration obtained in the last iteration.}
elements.sent-2411-229: Full details of the sampling parameters, including temperature and density ranges are provided in Appendix~\ref{app:al .}
elements.sent-2411-230: Only high-uncertainty configurations from the MD sampling simulations are included in the training dataset during al. To estimate the model uncertainty, an ensemble of eight ANI-type MLIPs was trained using randomized data splits (training, validation, test) and different initial model parameters.
elements.sent-2411-231: We employed ANI for AL because of the higher speeds for both learning in training and inference in sampling.
elements.sent-2411-232: The final training dataset used to train both ANI and HIP-NN potentials contains 12,336 atomic configurations, covering crystalline, defect-containing, and disordered structures.
elements.sent-2411-233: The generalized-gradient approximation (GGA)~\cite{Perdew1996 with projected-augmented wave (PAW)~\cite{Kresse1999 potentials was used to describe the exchange-correlation interactions.}}
elements.sent-2411-234: Spin-polarized calculations with ferromagnetic (FM) ordering on uranium atoms were performed as FM is energetically favorable with the PBE functional.
elements.sent-2411-235: Since the MLIPs will be applied to predict physical properties that are highly sensitive to lattice dynamics, we benchmarked the effect of exchange-correlation functionals by calculating the phonon dispersion of UN.
elements.sent-2411-236: Specifically, we compared the PBE and PBE+$U$~\cite{Dudarev1998 functionals (see Appendix~\ref{app:dft ).}}
elements.sent-2411-237: Among both, PBE was the only functional that did not yield imaginary (i.e., negative) phonon frequencies, indicating dynamic stability of the crystal structure.
elements.sent-2411-238: Consequently, PBE was selected as the most reliable and physically consistent choice for generating reference data to label our UN training set.
elements.sent-2411-239: Labeling calculations (energy and forces) used a $2 \times 2 \times 2$ supercell (64 atoms or 62 atoms for Schottky defect configurations) of the rock salt unit cell with periodic boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-240: We set a cutoff energy of 520 eV to expand the Kohn-Sham orbitals into the plane-wave basis sets, and the Methfessel-Paxton smearing method with width of 0.1 eV was applied~\cite{Methfessel1989 .}
elements.sent-2411-241: Convergence criteria were set to $10^{-8}$ eV for energy and $10^{-4}$ eV/\AA~for atomic forces, with the Brillouin zone sampled using a $3 \times 3 \times 3$ k-point grid.
elements.sent-2411-242: Molecular dynamics simulations for active learning were performed using the Atomic Simulation Environment (ASE)~\cite{ASE .}
elements.sent-2411-243: All other MD simulations were performed using LAMMPS~\cite{Thompson2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-244: Periodic boundary conditions were applied in all cases.
elements.sent-2411-245: Elastic constants and defect energetics at 0 K were computed using a $5 \times 5 \times 5$ supercell via finite deformation methods and nudged elastic band (NEB) calculations for migration barriers.
elements.sent-2411-246: Phonon spectra were calculated using the finite displacement method implemented in ASE using a $10 \times 10 \times 10$ supercell, and atomic displacements of 0.05 \AA~to compute the dynamical matrix.
elements.sent-2411-247: Finite-temperature properties were evaluated using a $15 \times 15 \times 15$ supercell under the isothermal-isobaric (NPT) ensemble, employing Nos\'e–Hoover thermostats and barostats with damping parameters set at 0.1 ps and 0.5 ps, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-248: Simulations spanned temperatures from 300~K to 2700~K at intervals of 25~K, each lasting 30 ps with a timestep of 1 fs.
elements.sent-2411-249: Properties such as lattice parameters, specific heat capacities--calculated from the temperature derivative of enthalpy-- and bulk moduli --determined from the derivative of pressure with respect to volume under compressive and tensile strains-- were derived from equilibrium averages over the final 10 ps of each run.
elements.sent-2411-250: Nitrogen self-diffusion was studied using the HIP-NN potential by analyzing the mean square displacements data from a 1-ns simulation under microcanonical (NVE) conditions at moderate to high temperatures (1800~K to 2800 K) followed initial equilibration under NPT conditions.

elements.sent-2411-257: Collision cascade simulations were performed using the HIP-NN potential for a large system containing approximately four million atoms ($80 \times 80 \times 80$ unit cells).
elements.sent-2411-258: Cascades were initiated by imparting a kinetic energy of 1 keV to a randomly oriented uranium primary knock-on atom (PKA).

elements.sent-2411-286: This consistency confirms that the vibrational baseline is well-represented and that the inclusion of DFT-based electronic corrections is essential for matching absolute values.
elements.sent-2411-287: At higher temperatures, experimental data reveal an enhanced heat capacity, attributed to thermally activated point defects.
elements.sent-2411-288: This behavior, often modeled via an exponential defect term, has been observed in several actinide materials, including UO$_2$ and UC, where defect generation becomes increasingly significant at elevated temperatures~\cite{Pavlov2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-289: In this high-temperature regime (above 1500~K), both MLIPs outperform the classical Kocevski potential.
elements.sent-2411-290: The HIP-NN model, in particular, shows excellent agreement with experimental data, successfully capturing the sharp rise in specific heat associated with defect contributions.
elements.sent-2411-291: The ANI potential also reproduces the increasing trend in heat capacity, though its agreement with experiment is somewhat less accurate than that of HIP-NN.
elements.sent-2411-292: Given that both models were trained on the same datasets, this discrepancy likely arises from differences in model architecture or hyperparameter optimization.

elements.subsection-2411-010: Defect migration barriers, diffusion, and Xe incorporation energies
elements.sent-2411-297: Accurate predictions of self-diffusion and impurity incorporation energies, such as Xenon, are crucial for understanding nuclear fuel behavior.
elements.sent-2411-298: These properties help elucidate the irradiation behavior of nuclear fuels.
elements.sent-2411-299: As the results for ANI and HIP-NN are quite similar, we focus this section solely on the HIP-NN model.
elements.sent-2411-300: NEB migration barriers for nitrogen and uranium defects: vacancies (vac) and interstitials (int) are given in Table~\ref{tab:neb .}
elements.sent-2411-301: Although our sampling scheme/dataset generation approach does not directly target migration barriers, the high-temperature simulations with interstitial defects in the initial structure are intended to explore these rare events.
elements.sent-2411-302: HIP-NN is able to provide reliable predictions that are in moderate agreement with the DFT reference data (calculated using PBE on a $2 \times 2 \times 2$ supercell).
elements.sent-2411-303: While improvement would require direct NEB sampling along migration pathways, the MLIP predicts a better migration barrier for U$_\text{int}$ than the classical potential of Kocevski et al.~\cite{Kocevski2022.}
elements.figure-2411-022: \caption{\label{tab:neb} Migration barriers (eV) for nitrogen (N) and uranium (U) vacancies (vac) and interstitials (int). For comparison, DFT (PBE) calculations~\cite{Kocevski2022} and classical MD predictions are included.} \begin{ruledtabular} \begin{tabular}{ccccc} \textbf{N$_\text{vac}$} \textbf{U$_\text{vac}$} \textbf{N$_\text{int}$} \textbf{U$_\text{int}$} HIP-NN 2.689 2.894 1.69 0.66 DFT (Ref.~\cite{Kocevski2022}) 2.983 3.396 2.422 0.976 Kocevski 2.524 3.363 2.012 0.28 \end{tabular} \end{ruledtabular}
elements.sent-2411-304: We investigate nitrogen self-diffusion in both stoichiometric and hypo-stoichiometric UN using the HIP-NN potential.
elements.sent-2411-305: Diffusion coefficients computed across a wide temperature range are summarized in Fig.~\ref{fig:dif .}
elements.sent-2411-306: In stoichiometric UN, measurable diffusion only emerges above 2300~K, consistent with the expected high migration barrier for nitrogen in a defect-free lattice.
elements.sent-2411-307: To explore the role of point defects, we study two hypo-stoichiometric compositions with nitrogen vacancy concentrations of 0.5\% and 1\%, as well as a hyper-stoichiometric case with 0.5\% nitrogen interstitials.
elements.sent-2411-308: Compared to pure UN, both vacancy concentrations yielded systematically higher diffusivities and significantly lower activation energies.
elements.sent-2411-309: The activation energy for nitrogen diffusion in hypo-stoichiometric UN at low temperatures was found to be 2.82~eV for 0.5\% vacancies and 2.57~eV for 1\% vacancies.
elements.sent-2411-310: As expected, increasing the vacancy concentration lowers the barrier due to a vacancy-assisted hopping mechanism.
elements.figure-2411-023: \includegraphics[width=0.65\linewidth]{diffusion.pdf} \caption{\label{fig:dif} Nitrogen self-diffusion vs inverse temperature using HIP-NN for an stoichiometric UN, a system with 0.5\% nitrogen vacancies and 1\% nitrogen vacancies, and a UN system with 0.5\% nitrogen interstitials. Error bars are standard deviation from eight simulations with distinct ensemble members.}
elements.sent-2411-311: The hyper-stoichiometric interstitial system exhibited even greater enhancement.
elements.sent-2411-312: The much lower activation energy of 0.97~eV indicates a fundamentally different and more efficient diffusion mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-313: At higher temperatures, there is an increased statistical uncertainty between MLIP ensemble members, likely reflecting the larger configurational space accessible to nitrogen interstitials and their potential to undergo correlated or burst-like migration events.
elements.sent-2411-314: The diffusion results demonstrate that nitrogen transport in UN is strongly facilitated by both vacancy and interstitial defects, with interstitials emerging as the dominant diffusion pathway under non-stoichiometric conditions.
elements.sent-2411-315: This trend aligns with experimental observations suggesting that self-diffusion in uranium mononitride proceeds primarily via interstitial-mediated mechanisms\cite{Holt1969, Matzke1990 .}
elements.sent-2411-316: At high temperatures (above 2500~K), the diffusion coefficients of all systems--stoichiometric and non-stoichiometric--begin to converge.
elements.sent-2411-317: This convergence suggests that thermal energy becomes sufficient to overcome even high migration barriers, activating diffusion pathways regardless of initial defect concentration.
elements.figure-2411-024: \caption{\label{tab:xe} Incorporation energies of Xenon (eV) in UN in comparison to reference DFT and classical potential data provided by~\cite{Kocevski2022}.} \begin{ruledtabular} \begin{tabular}{cccc} \textbf{Xe$_\text{int}$} \textbf{Xe$_\text{N}$} \textbf{Xe$_\text{U}$} HIP-NN 16.85 8.96 4.0 DFT (Ref.~\cite{Kocevski2022}) 14.68 8.45 4.84 Kocevski 16.39 7.89 3.96 \end{tabular} \end{ruledtabular}
elements.sent-2411-318: While studying fuel swelling was not the focus of this study, we report the Xe incorporation energies for different sites in Table~\ref{tab:xe .}
elements.sent-2411-319: Our predicted incorporation energies of Xe into a nitrogen vacancy (Xe$_\text{N}$) and a uranium vacancy (Xe$_\text{U}$) are in excellent agreement with reference DFT (PBE) data calculated by Kocevski et al.~\cite{Kocevski2023 However, the incorporation energies for a Xe atom at the tetrahedral interstitial site (Xe$_\text{int}$) are overestimated by more than 2 eV with our hybrid HIP-NN and Buckingham potential approach.}
elements.sent-2411-320: While the accuracy of the incorporation energies are likely limited in accuracy by the Buckingham potential used for Xe, these results demonstrate that our MLIP is compatible with simple classical potentials for simulating noble gas impurities.
elements.sent-2411-321: Cascade simulations are widely used to investigate the atomic-scale effects of irradiation in materials.
elements.sent-2411-322: Although detailed irradiation damage studies were not the primary objective of this work, we demonstrate that our HIP-NN potential is capable of performing large-scale cascade simulations for modeling defect formation processes in UN.
elements.sent-2411-323: We simulate a 1 keV collision cascade initiated by a uranium primary knock-on atom (PKA).
elements.sent-2411-324: The impact triggers a sequence of atomic displacements and local disorder.
elements.sent-2411-325: We monitor the evolution of point defects over time, focusing on the formation of Frenkel pairs--composed of interstitial-vacancy pairs--as a key indicator of damage.
elements.sent-2411-326: Figure~\ref{fig:cascade shows representative snapshots of the cascade evolution.}
elements.sent-2411-327: At 0.2 picoseconds after impact, significant Frenkel pair formation occurs near the PKA origin.
elements.sent-2411-328: The number of defects peaks around 2 picoseconds and then gradually declines as atoms recombine or return to lattice positions.
elements.sent-2411-329: By several tens of picoseconds, the system reaches a steady state, with the surviving defect population stabilized.
elements.sent-2411-330: While this single simulation is not intended as an in-depth study of radiation effects, it provides evidence that our MLIP can serve a broader applicability for modeling radiation damage.
elements.sent-2411-331: We present two machine learning interatomic potentials that can describe uranium mononitride for studying atomic-scale phenomenon and for predicting physical properties.
elements.sent-2411-332: As shown in previous studies, developing a machine learning interatomic potential for nuclear fuels can be a challenging task, especially for actinide compounds.
elements.sent-2411-333: First, each potential was validated with the 300~K lattice parameter, 0~K elastic constants, 0~K bulk modulus and phonon spectra.
elements.sent-2411-334: Further validation investigated the temperature-dependent lattice parameter, specific heat capacity and bulk modulus over a temperature range from 300~K to 2700~K.
elements.sent-2411-335: Both potentials are capable of accurately reproducing DFT-MD simulation results and experimental data, when available.
elements.sent-2411-336: The potentials were also validated by evaluating defect energies, key for a good understanding of the nuclear fuel behavior under irradiation.
elements.sent-2411-337: The HIP-NN trained potential was also used to calculate migration barriers for several defects.
elements.sent-2411-338: Despite these rare events not being included in the training dataset, HIP-NN predictions show reasonable agreement with DFT calculations.
elements.sent-2411-339: HIP-NN was also used to study diffusion coefficients and diffusion mechanisms, achieving results consistent with experiment.
elements.sent-2411-340: Xenon incorporation energies, calculated with a hybrid HIP-NN and Buckingham potential model, are also in close agreement with DFT.
elements.sent-2411-341: Finally, we demonstrate the capability of HIP-NN to perform large-scale cascade simulations.
elements.sent-2411-342: Future work will explore additional properties, such as vibrational entropy, to further enhance multiscale simulation capabilities for fuel performance.
elements.sent-2411-343: We also anticipate applying other techniques, such as experimental refinement to improve the agreement with experiment.
elements.sent-2411-344: We also appreciate the resources provided by the LANL Institutional Computing (IC) program.. \end{acknowledgments.}

elements.sent-2411-346: The neural networks for U and N are composed of three hidden layers with 96, 96, and 64 nodes, using the CELU activation function and a linear output layer.
elements.sent-2411-347: The radial component employed a cutoff of 7.0 \AA, with 32 Gaussian radial basis functions centered between 1.25 \AA~and 6.82 \AA.
elements.sent-2411-348: The angular descriptor used a radial cutoff of 5.0 \AA, with 64 basis functions composed of 8 radial shifts and 8 angular shifts, distributed across interatomic distances and angles.
elements.sent-2411-349: ANI ensemble was trained using both energy and force labels, with respective loss weights of 1.0 and 0.1.. HIP-NN potential.
elements.sent-2411-350: In this work, we found that a single interaction layer was sufficient ($n_interaction=1$).
elements.sent-2411-351: We used the improved interaction HIP-NN form that includes tensor sensitivities~\cite{Chigaev2023 by setting the tensor order ${\ell =1$, that is, vector-based message passing, which improves over the original HIP-NN by summing messages generated from neighbors not only as scalars but also as vectors.}}
elements.sent-2411-352: Trained was done using loss weights of 1.0 for both energy and force labels.
elements.sent-2411-353: Both temperature and density were varied dynamically throughout the simulations using a combination of linear ramping and sinusoidal modulation, following work done in Ref.~\cite{Smith2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-354: This approach allowed the system to explore a broad range of equilibrium and non-equilibrium states relevant to uranium nitride.
elements.sent-2411-355: At each MD time step \( t \), the temperature \( T(t) \) was updated as follows: \begin{equation}T(t) = T_{start} + \left( \frac{t}{t_{max}} \right)(T_{end} - T_{start}) + T_{amp} \cdot \sin^2\left( \frac{t}{t_{per}} \right)\end{equation} The number density \( N/V(t) \) was modulated in an analogous manner: \begin{equation}\frac{N}{V(t)} = \left( \frac{N}{V} \right)_{start} + \left( \frac{t}{t_{max}} \right)\left[\left( \frac{N}{V} \right)_{end} - \left( \frac{N}{V} \right)_{start} \right] + \left( \frac{N}{V} \right)_{amp} \cdot \sin^2\left( \frac{t}{t_{per}} \right)\end{equation} Density changes were implemented by scaling the simulation cell volume and atom positions by the ratio between the target and previous volume.
elements.sent-2411-356: All MD simulations were carried out using the ASE Langevin thermostat with a timestep of 1.0~fs.
elements.sent-2411-357: Configuration snapshots were extracted every 10 steps.
elements.sent-2411-358: Only configurations with an ensemble uncertainty higher than the energy or force thresholds are stored in the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-359: We employed a threshold for the uncertainty in total energy of 28 eV/atom, for the mean uncertainty in force of 0.088 eV/\AA~, and for the maximum uncertainty in force of 0.024 eV/\AA.~The specific parameters used are summarized in Table~\ref{tab:sampling_params .}

elements.section-2411-010: Validation of PBE functional: phonon bands
elements.sent-2411-360: Previous DFT calculation have shown that PBE+U can reproduce the UN antiferromagnetic ordering seen in experiments at low temperatures.
elements.sent-2411-361: However, when using the Hubbard parameter, dynamical instabilities can be introduced in UN, manifesting itself as imaginary frequencies in the phonon spectra~\cite{Kocevski2022a .}
elements.sent-2411-362: To validate our functional, we calculated the phonon dispersions for a ferromagnetic uranium nitride on a $2 \times 2 \times 2$ supercell using PBE and PBE+U with U=1.85 eV functionals.
elements.sent-2411-363: The results are shown in Fig.~\ref{appa , where one can observe the appearance of imaginary phonons with the PBE+U functional.}
